editableNewDialogue.text: Quick drafting question: in an email, is it better to say “by EOD tomorrow” or “by close of business tomorrow”?

“Close of business tomorrow” is usually clearer in a professional context, but it can still be ambiguous across time zones. If timing matters, the clearest option is to specify a time and time zone (e.g., “by 5:00 p.m. ET tomorrow”). “EOD tomorrow” is common internally, but it can read informal or unclear to external recipients.
If I keep “EOD,” what’s a clean way to define it in the same message without sounding stiff?

You can define it once, lightly, in parentheses. For example: “Could you please send this by EOD tomorrow (11:59 p.m. your local time)?” or “...by EOD tomorrow (5:00 p.m. ET).” If you want to avoid “local time” ambiguity, pick a specific time zone.
Separate question: in markets headlines, what does it mean when a currency “pares gains”?

“Pares gains” means it’s still up on the day/period, but less than it was earlier—i.e., it gave back part of its earlier rise. Example: if a currency was up 1.2% earlier and later is up 0.4%, it has “pared gains.”
Got it. Also, I saw a note saying a bond was “trading on a price-yield basis.” What’s that getting at?

It’s pointing to the inverse relationship between price and yield and how quotes/analysis may switch between the two. Traders may talk in price terms (e.g., 98.50) or yield terms (e.g., 5.10%). “Price-yield basis” often just signals the bond is being quoted/valued with that linkage in mind, and that small price moves map into yield moves.
Random trivia: why do some old maps say “Here be dragons”?

“Here be dragons” is a shorthand for warning of unknown or dangerous areas on maps. Historically it’s more famous than common—only a few artifacts use variants of it (a well-known example is the Latin “Hic sunt dracones” on a 16th-century globe)—but the broader idea of marking unexplored regions with monsters or hazards was widespread in early cartography.
On the yen point—if USD/JPY drops from 142 to 130 and someone says “yen hit a three‑month high,” that’s 円高, right?

Yes—if USD/JPY moves from 142 down to 130, the yen is strengthening (you need fewer yen to buy $1), which is 円高. “Hit a three-month high” refers to the yen’s value being relatively strong versus the dollar compared with the prior three months.
Quick correction on the note you have: I never said anything about not retaining addresses or passport info. We were just talking about interview questions and some finance/FX terms.
You're right—there’s nothing in what you asked that mentions any preference about keeping or not keeping addresses/passport information. This chat doesn’t contain a data-retention request from you.
